turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show! Today we're diving into AI-assisted medical imaging — a field that's transforming how doctors read X-rays and scans. To start us off, why does radiology need AI in the first place?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: A single hospital can produce tens of thousands of scans daily, and reading them is exhausting work subject to fatigue and perceptual error. AI doesn't get tired — it offers consistent, second-pair-of-eyes scrutiny at a scale humans simply can't sustain.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's fascinating — so the AI is essentially a tireless assistant. But how does it actually "see" these images? What's happening under the hood when a neural network looks at a chest X-ray?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of a CNN like a radiologist scanning with a magnifying glass, building from edges to shapes layer by layer. CheXNet used this approach—a DenseNet-121 model trained on

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Over a hundred thousand images, right? That's incredible. So CheXNet actually matched radiologist-level performance — but I've also heard about something called vision transformers. How do those differ from CNNs?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: If a CNN is a magnifying glass scanning locally, a vision transformer steps back and sees the whole picture at once. It divides the image into patches and uses self-attention to capture global context immediately — crucial for something like assessing cardiomegaly, where you need to compare heart size to overall thoracic width.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That makes so much sense — some findings need that big-picture view. So with CNNs and transformers

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Both available, researchers are now building hybrid systems that combine CNNs' local feature extraction with transformers' global attention—like giving our radiologist both a magnifying glass and wide-angle lens. These hybrids are already outperforming pure CNNs on tasks like lung nodule classification.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So we've gone from magnifying glass to wide-angle lens — it's remarkable how far this has come. Before we wrap up, what do you think is the biggest hurdle still standing between these tools and everyday clinical use?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: The biggest hurdle is that sensitivity and specificity are always in tension. You can tune a model to catch nearly every pneumonia case, but then you flood radiologists with false alarms. Finding that clinical sweet spot—where the AI truly helps without overwhelming the workflow—is remarkably difficult.
